config.title: Berlin U-Bahn Etiquette: FAQ
config.subtitle: Plain answers on Berlin U-Bahn doors, escalators, seats, food, bikes and ticket checks, separating current VBB rules from local habits.

items.q: What are the unwritten rules of the Berlin U-Bahn?
items.a: Berlin U-Bahn etiquette mixes written transport rules with local habits. Wait at the sides of the doors, let people off, then board and move inside. Stand on the right of the escalator and leave the left clear, press the door button when it lights, keep sound to yourself, leave priority seats for passengers who need them, and carry the correct ticket.

items.q: Do Berlin U-Bahn doors open automatically?
items.a: Often no. On many U-Bahn and S-Bahn trains you have to open the door yourself by pressing the illuminated button or lifting the handle. The button lights up once the train releases the doors at a stop; press it then. If you stand and wait for the door to slide open on its own, you can watch your stop go by.

items.q: Which side of the escalator do you stand on in Berlin?
items.a: Stand on the right, single file, and leave the left side clear for people walking up or down. The familiar phrase is "rechts stehen, links gehen". At busy interchanges such as Alexanderplatz and Friedrichstraße, keep your suitcase on the right with you rather than across the left lane.

items.q: Can you eat and drink on the Berlin U-Bahn?
items.a: Keep food and drinks closed on board. Current VBB conditions prohibit entering a vehicle with open food or an open drink and consuming them during the journey. That includes open beer. Since 1 May 2026, a DB pilot also prohibits consuming alcohol or carrying it open at Zoologischer Garten and Ostbahnhof, including their forecourts, apart from licensed shops and restaurants.

items.q: Can I take a bike on the U-Bahn in Berlin?
items.a: Yes. A bike normally needs its own bike ticket in addition to your passenger ticket. Use a door marked with the bicycle symbol and the designated area. Wheelchair users and passengers with prams have priority, and staff can ask you to wait for another train when it is full. Current BVG and VBB guidance does not publish a blanket weekday rush-hour ban. Bicycles are not carried on BVG buses.

items.q: Do I really need to validate my ticket in Berlin?
items.a: Yes. Berlin U-Bahn and S-Bahn platforms are open with no barriers, but inspectors still check trains. If a paper ticket requires stamping, validate it before the journey. Travelling without a valid ticket can lead to a €60 increased transport charge, called an EBE; the inspector gives you a receipt with the number used for payment.

items.q: Are Berliners rude on public transport?
items.a: Rudeness is the wrong way to frame it. A busy train depends on passengers leaving doorways and aisles clear, while VBB rules also prohibit sound that disturbs others. Wait at the side of the doors, move into the carriage and use headphones; those concrete actions matter more than guessing what every Berliner thinks.
